name: Curcumin
description: A natural yellow colouring derived from turmeric, widely considered safe.
detailed_description: Curcumin is a naturally occurring bright yellow pigment extracted from the rhizome of the turmeric plant (Curcuma longa). It is commonly found in mustard, dairy products, ice cream, and confectionery to provide yellow-orange colour. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is widely regarded as one of the safest food colourings available.

name: Riboflavin
description: Vitamin B2 — a natural yellow colouring and essential nutrient, considered very safe.
detailed_description: Riboflavin, also known as Vitamin B2, is an essential water-soluble nutrient that also serves as a yellow food colouring. It is found naturally in milk, eggs, and green vegetables, and is added to cereals, baby foods, and processed cheese for both nutritional fortification and colour. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered very safe as it is a necessary vitamin for human health.

name: Tartrazine
description: A synthetic yellow dye linked to hyperactivity in children and allergic reactions.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — mandatory warning label
detailed_description: Tartrazine is a synthetic lemon-yellow azo dye widely used in soft drinks, confectionery, sauces, and snack foods. It is one of the six 'Southampton dyes' that were the focus of a 2007 UK Food Standards Agency study on hyperactivity in children. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but requires a warning label stating it 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in the 2007 McCann et al. Southampton study
May trigger allergic-type reactions, particularly in individuals sensitive to aspirin
Some research suggests it may exacerbate asthma symptoms in susceptible individuals

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Quinoline Yellow
description: A synthetic yellow-green dye associated with hyperactivity in children.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — mandatory warning label
detailed_description: Quinoline Yellow is a synthetic greenish-yellow dye used in soft drinks, confectionery, and smoked fish. It is one of the six Southampton dyes identified in research linking artificial colourings to hyperactivity in children. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but must carry a warning label about potential effects on activity and attention in children.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in some studies
May trigger allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
Some research suggests potential genotoxicity, though EFSA found insufficient evidence to change its approval

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Sunset Yellow
description: A synthetic orange-yellow dye linked to hyperactivity in children.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — mandatory warning label
detailed_description: Sunset Yellow FCF is a synthetic orange-yellow azo dye commonly found in orange squash, marmalade, confectionery, and packet soups. It is one of the six Southampton dyes studied for links to hyperactivity in children. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but requires a warning label regarding potential effects on activity and attention in children.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in the 2007 Southampton study
May trigger allergic-type reactions, particularly in people with aspirin sensitivity
Some studies suggest it may worsen symptoms of asthma and urticaria in sensitive individuals

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of Sunset Yellow FCF (E 110)
Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Cochineal
description: A natural red dye made from crushed insects, may cause allergic reactions in some people.
detailed_description: Cochineal (carminic acid) is a natural red colouring derived from the dried bodies of the female cochineal insect (Dactylopius coccus). It is commonly found in yoghurts, confectionery, drinks, and processed meats to provide red-pink colouring. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some individuals may experience allergic reactions, and it is unsuitable for vegetarians and vegans.
potential_risks: Some individuals may experience allergic reactions including anaphylaxis in rare cases
Linked to occupational asthma in workers handling the raw material

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of cochineal, carminic acid, carmines (E 120)

name: Carmoisine
description: A synthetic red dye associated with hyperactivity in children.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — mandatory warning label
detailed_description: Carmoisine (Azorubine) is a synthetic red azo dye used in blancmange, marzipan, Swiss rolls, jams, and confectionery. It is one of the six Southampton dyes identified in research on hyperactivity in children. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but requires a warning label about potential effects on activity and attention in children.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in the 2007 Southampton study
May trigger allergic reactions in aspirin-sensitive individuals
Some research suggests potential links to skin rashes in sensitive individuals

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Ponceau 4R
description: A synthetic red dye linked to hyperactivity in children and banned in some countries.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — mandatory warning label
detailed_description: Ponceau 4R is a synthetic red azo dye used in dessert toppings, tinned fruit, salami, and confectionery. It is one of the six Southampton dyes and is banned in the United States and Norway due to safety concerns. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but requires a warning label about potential effects on activity and attention in children.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in the 2007 Southampton study
Banned in the USA and several other countries due to safety concerns
May trigger allergic-type reactions in aspirin-sensitive individuals

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Allura Red
description: A synthetic red dye that may cause allergic reactions and is restricted in some countries.
detailed_description: Allura Red AC is a synthetic red azo dye commonly used in soft drinks, sweets, cake mixes, and sauces. It is one of the six Southampton dyes studied for effects on child behaviour, though the evidence for this additive specifically is less consistent. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and requires a warning label in the EU.
potential_risks: Associated with hyperactivity in children in some studies, though evidence is debated
May trigger allergic reactions in sensitive individuals, particularly those with aspirin intolerance

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of Allura Red AC (E 129)

uk_notes: Requires mandatory warning label in UK: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'. Required under UK Food Information Regulations 2014.

name: Patent Blue V
description: A synthetic blue dye that may cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.
detailed_description: Patent Blue V is a synthetic blue dye used in confectionery, baked goods, and as a diagnostic dye in medicine. It is not permitted for food use in the United States or Australia. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though allergic reactions including anaphylaxis have been reported in rare cases, primarily from medical diagnostic use.
potential_risks: Some individuals may experience allergic reactions, including rare cases of anaphylaxis reported in medical settings
Not approved for food use in several countries including the USA and Australia

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of Patent Blue V (E 131)

name: Brilliant Blue
description: A synthetic blue dye generally considered safe in approved quantities.
detailed_description: Brilliant Blue FCF is a synthetic blue dye used in confectionery, ice cream, canned peas, and dairy products. It is one of the better-studied synthetic dyes and is approved in both the EU and the USA. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is generally considered safe at normal dietary intake levels.

name: Caramel I (Plain)
description: Simple caramel colouring made by heating sugar, considered safe.
detailed_description: Caramel I (Plain Caramel) is the simplest form of caramel colouring, made by controlled heating of sugar without the use of ammonium or sulphite compounds. It is found in a wide range of foods including bakery products, sauces, and confectionery. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered the safest of the four caramel colouring classes.

name: Caramel II
description: Caramel colouring made with sulphite, some concerns about byproducts.
detailed_description: Caramel II (Caustic Sulphite Caramel) is produced by heating sugars in the presence of sulphite compounds. It is used in cognac, sherry, and some vinegars. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some researchers have raised concerns about sulphite-related byproducts formed during its manufacture.
potential_risks: Contains sulphite residues which may trigger reactions in sulphite-sensitive individuals
Some concerns about processing byproducts, though levels in food are generally low

sources.title: EFSA scientific opinion on the re-evaluation of caramel colours (E 150a, b, c, d)

name: Caramel III
description: Caramel colouring made with ammonia, some concerns about byproducts.
detailed_description: Caramel III (Ammonia Caramel) is produced by heating sugars with ammonia compounds, and is commonly used in beer, soy sauce, and bakery products. The ammonia process can produce a byproduct called 2-methylimidazole (2-MEI), which some research has flagged as a concern. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, and EFSA has set a group ADI for caramel colours.
potential_risks: Production process may generate 2-methylimidazole (2-MEI), which some studies have associated with concerns in animal models
Some research suggests potential immunotoxic effects at high doses in animal studies

sources.title: EFSA scientific opinion on the re-evaluation of caramel colours (E 150a, b, c, d)

name: Caramel IV
description: The most processed caramel colouring, contains compounds some researchers flag as potentially concerning.
detailed_description: Caramel IV (Sulphite Ammonia Caramel) is the most widely used caramel colouring, produced using both sulphite and ammonia compounds. It is found extensively in cola drinks, dark breads, sauces, and gravy products. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though it has attracted the most scrutiny of the four caramel classes due to the byproduct 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI).
potential_risks: Production may generate 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), which California's Proposition 65 lists as a potential carcinogen
Contains both sulphite and ammonia-derived byproducts
Some animal studies suggest potential immunotoxic effects at high doses, though EFSA considers current dietary exposure safe

sources.title: EFSA scientific opinion on the re-evaluation of caramel colours (E 150a, b, c, d)

name: Beta-Carotene
description: A natural orange pigment found in carrots and other vegetables, converts to Vitamin A in the body.
detailed_description: Beta-carotene is a natural orange-red pigment abundant in carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach, and other fruits and vegetables. It is used as a food colouring in margarine, cheese, and beverages, and the body converts it into vitamin A as needed. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered very safe as a food additive.

name: Annatto
description: A natural orange-red dye from seeds that can occasionally cause allergic reactions.
detailed_description: Annatto (bixin, norbixin) is a natural orange-red colouring extracted from the seeds of the achiote tree (Bixa orellana). It is commonly used in cheese (such as Red Leicester and Cheddar), margarine, and snack foods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though it can occasionally cause allergic or intolerance reactions in some individuals.
potential_risks: Some individuals may experience allergic or intolerance reactions, though this is relatively uncommon
Reported cases of urticaria and angioedema in sensitive individuals

name: Paprika Extract
description: A natural red-orange colouring extracted from paprika peppers, considered safe.
detailed_description: Paprika extract (capsanthin, capsorubin) is a natural red-orange colouring obtained from the fruits of Capsicum annuum peppers. It is commonly found in sauces, snack foods, processed cheese, and meat products. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is generally considered safe as a food colouring.

name: Calcium Carbonate
description: Chalk — used as a white colouring and calcium supplement, completely safe.
detailed_description: Calcium carbonate is a naturally occurring mineral (chalk/limestone) used as a white colouring agent, acidity regulator, and calcium supplement. It is found in bread (added to white flour by law in the UK), confectionery, and dietary supplements. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered completely safe at normal dietary levels.

name: Titanium Dioxide
description: A white pigment used as a food colouring.
detailed_description: Titanium dioxide is a white pigment used in sweets, chewing gum, white sauces, and icing. The EU banned it in food in August 2022 following EFSA's conclusion that it could not be considered safe due to potential genotoxicity from nanoparticles. The UK FSA reviewed the same evidence independently and decided to maintain its permitted status, concluding the evidence was not sufficient to withdraw authorisation.
potential_risks: Potential genotoxicity (EU assessment)
Banned in EU food since 2022
UK and EU have diverged on this additive

sources.title: EFSA safety assessment of titanium dioxide (E 171)
UK FSA statement on titanium dioxide

uk_notes: Currently permitted in GB following independent FSA review. The FSA concluded the evidence for genotoxicity was not sufficient to withdraw authorisation.

name: Sorbic Acid
description: A naturally occurring preservative found in berries, considered very safe.
detailed_description: Sorbic acid is a naturally occurring organic acid first isolated from the berries of the rowan tree (Sorbus aucuparia). It is widely used as a preservative in cheese, wine, baked goods, and personal care products to inhibit moulds and yeasts. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered one of the safest food preservatives available.

name: Potassium Sorbate
description: A common preservative that prevents mould growth, considered safe.
detailed_description: Potassium sorbate is the potassium salt of sorbic acid, widely used as a preservative to inhibit mould and yeast growth in foods. It is commonly found in cheese, yoghurt, wine, dried meats, and soft drinks. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, being metabolised by the body in the same way as naturally occurring fatty acids.

name: Sodium Benzoate
description: A preservative that can form benzene when combined with vitamin C, linked to hyperactivity.
detailed_description: Sodium benzoate is a widely used synthetic preservative effective against yeasts, moulds, and some bacteria. It is commonly found in soft drinks, fruit juices, pickles, and sauces. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, but concerns have been raised about its potential to form benzene (a known carcinogen) when combined with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) in acidic beverages, and it was included in the 2007 Southampton study on hyperactivity.
potential_risks: Can form benzene, a known carcinogen, when combined with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) in acidic conditions
Associated with hyperactivity in children in the 2007 Southampton study when combined with certain colourings
Some individuals may experience allergic-type reactions including urticaria and asthma exacerbation

sources.title: EFSA opinion on the safety of benzoic acid and its salts
Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (McCann et al.)
UK FSA survey of benzene in soft drinks

name: Sulphur Dioxide
description: A preservative commonly used in dried fruits and wine, can trigger asthma symptoms.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — allergen labelling required
detailed_description: Sulphur dioxide is a gaseous preservative and antioxidant used to prevent browning and microbial spoilage in a wide range of foods. It is commonly found in dried fruits, wine, fruit juices, and processed potatoes. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but is classified as an allergen requiring mandatory labelling, as it can trigger adverse reactions in sulphite-sensitive individuals, particularly asthmatics.
potential_risks: Can trigger bronchoconstriction and asthma symptoms in sulphite-sensitive individuals
May cause headaches, flushing, and gastrointestinal symptoms in sensitive people
Classified as an allergen under EU food labelling regulations

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of sulphur dioxide and sulphites (E 220-228)

name: Sodium Metabisulphite
description: A sulphite preservative that can trigger breathing difficulties in asthmatics.
regulation: UK Food Information Regulations 2014 — allergen labelling required
detailed_description: Sodium metabisulphite is a sulphite-based preservative and antioxidant used to prevent browning and inhibit bacterial growth. It is commonly found in wine, beer, dried fruits, and processed seafood. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits but must be declared on labels as a sulphite allergen, as it can trigger respiratory symptoms in susceptible individuals, particularly those with asthma.
potential_risks: Can trigger bronchoconstriction and breathing difficulties in sulphite-sensitive asthmatics
May cause allergic-type reactions including skin rashes and gastrointestinal distress in sensitive individuals

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of sulphur dioxide and sulphites (E 220-228)

name: Potassium Nitrite
description: A curing agent in processed meats linked to formation of potentially harmful nitrosamines.
detailed_description: Potassium nitrite is a preservative and curing agent used primarily in processed and cured meats such as ham, bacon, and sausages, where it inhibits the growth of Clostridium botulinum (botulism). It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits. Some research has linked nitrite-cured meats to the formation of nitrosamines, which are associated with increased risk in epidemiological studies, though the additive also serves an important food safety function.
potential_risks: Can react with amines in meat to form nitrosamines, which some studies associate with increased cancer risk
Epidemiological studies have linked high consumption of nitrite-cured processed meats to colorectal cancer risk
IARC classifies processed meat as a Group 1 carcinogen, partly due to nitrosamine formation

sources.title: EFSA opinion on nitrites in meat products

uk_notes: Permitted only in cured and preserved meats at specified maximum levels under UK assimilated law.

name: Sodium Nitrite
description: Used in processed meats for colour and preservation, linked to formation of harmful nitrosamines.
detailed_description: Sodium nitrite is one of the most widely used curing agents in processed meats including bacon, ham, hot dogs, and salami, where it prevents botulism and gives the characteristic pink colour. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and serves a critical food safety function. However, some research has linked it to the formation of nitrosamines during high-temperature cooking, and epidemiological studies associate high processed meat consumption with increased health risks.
potential_risks: Can form nitrosamines when heated, particularly during frying or grilling of cured meats
Epidemiological studies associate high intake of nitrite-cured processed meats with increased colorectal cancer risk
IARC classifies processed meat as a Group 1 carcinogen, with nitrosamines as a contributing factor
The UK FSA continues to monitor research on nitrite levels in food

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of sodium nitrite (E 250) and potassium nitrite (E 249)
Red meat and processed meat - IARC Monographs
UK FSA guidance on nitrites in food

uk_notes: Permitted only in cured and preserved meats at specified maximum levels under UK assimilated law.

name: Sodium Nitrate
description: A preservative in cured meats that can convert to nitrites, linked to health concerns.
detailed_description: Sodium nitrate is a preservative used in cured and processed meats such as salami, chorizo, and some traditionally cured hams. It acts as a reservoir that slowly converts to sodium nitrite during the curing process. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, but shares the same concerns as sodium nitrite regarding nitrosamine formation, particularly when cured meats are cooked at high temperatures.
potential_risks: Converts to nitrite in the body and in cured meat, with associated nitrosamine formation concerns
Epidemiological studies link high consumption of nitrate/nitrite-cured meats to increased colorectal cancer risk
Some studies suggest associations with other health outcomes, though causation is not established

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of sodium nitrate (E 251) and potassium nitrate (E 252)

uk_notes: Permitted only in cured and preserved meats at specified maximum levels under UK assimilated law.

name: Acetic Acid
description: Simply vinegar — a natural preservative and flavouring, completely safe.
detailed_description: Acetic acid is the primary component of vinegar and has been used as a food preservative and flavouring for thousands of years. It is found in pickled vegetables, condiments, sauces, and baked goods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered completely safe at the levels used in food.

name: Lactic Acid
description: A naturally occurring acid found in fermented foods like yoghurt, very safe.
detailed_description: Lactic acid is a naturally occurring organic acid produced during the fermentation of lactose by lactic acid bacteria. It is found in yoghurt, sauerkraut, sourdough bread, and is used as an acidity regulator and preservative in many processed foods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered very safe, as it is naturally produced in the human body during exercise.

name: Calcium Propionate
description: A mould inhibitor used in bread, some studies link it to behavioural changes in children.
detailed_description: Calcium propionate is a preservative primarily used in bread and bakery products to inhibit mould and bacterial growth, extending shelf life. It is one of the most common bread preservatives worldwide. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some research, including a notable Australian study, has suggested associations with behavioural changes in some children.
potential_risks: Some studies suggest associations with irritability, restlessness, and sleep disturbance in children
An Australian study linked it to behavioural changes, though the evidence base is limited

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of propionic acid and its salts (E 280-283)

name: Malic Acid
description: A naturally occurring acid found in apples and other fruits, considered safe.
detailed_description: Malic acid is a naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid found in apples, grapes, and many other fruits. It is used as a flavouring agent and acidity regulator in confectionery, fruit-flavoured drinks, and jams. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, as it is a normal intermediate in human metabolism.

name: Ascorbic Acid
description: Vitamin C — a natural antioxidant that is beneficial to health.
detailed_description: Ascorbic acid is Vitamin C, an essential nutrient and powerful natural antioxidant. It is used in fruit juices, cured meats, bread, and many other products to prevent oxidation and preserve freshness. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered entirely safe, providing nutritional benefit as an essential vitamin.

name: Mixed Tocopherols
description: A mixture of natural Vitamin E compounds used as an antioxidant, considered safe and beneficial.
detailed_description: Mixed tocopherols are a combination of naturally occurring forms of Vitamin E extracted from vegetable oils. They are used as antioxidants in fats, oils, cereals, and snack foods to prevent rancidity. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe and beneficial, as Vitamin E is an essential nutrient.

name: Alpha-Tocopherol
description: Synthetic Vitamin E used as an antioxidant to prevent fats from going rancid, considered safe.
detailed_description: Alpha-tocopherol is the most biologically active form of Vitamin E, used as an antioxidant to prevent oxidation of fats and oils in food products. It is found in vegetable oils, margarine, and processed foods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, functioning both as a food preservative and a beneficial nutrient.

name: Gamma-Tocopherol
description: A form of Vitamin E used as an antioxidant, considered safe.
detailed_description: Gamma-tocopherol is a form of Vitamin E used as a natural antioxidant in food products to prevent fat oxidation. It is found naturally in soybean oil, corn oil, and various nuts. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe as both a food additive and a naturally occurring nutrient.

name: Delta-Tocopherol
description: A form of Vitamin E used as an antioxidant, considered safe.
detailed_description: Delta-tocopherol is a form of Vitamin E used as a natural antioxidant in food to prevent rancidity of fats and oils. It is found naturally in soybean oil and rice bran oil. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe as a food antioxidant.

name: Sodium Erythorbate
description: An antioxidant used in cured meats to speed up curing and preserve colour, considered safe.
detailed_description: Sodium erythorbate is a synthetic antioxidant chemically related to ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), used primarily in cured meats to accelerate the curing process and maintain colour. It is commonly found in hot dogs, sausages, and deli meats. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe at the levels used in food.

name: Lecithins
description: A natural emulsifier found in egg yolks and soybeans, considered safe.
detailed_description: Lecithins are a group of naturally occurring phospholipids found abundantly in egg yolks, soybeans, and sunflower seeds. They are one of the most widely used emulsifiers in the food industry, found in chocolate, margarine, baked goods, and instant foods. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe, being a natural component of cell membranes.

name: Citric Acid
description: A natural acid found in citrus fruits, widely used and very safe.
detailed_description: Citric acid is a naturally occurring weak organic acid found in high concentrations in citrus fruits such as lemons and limes. It is one of the most widely used food additives, found in soft drinks, confectionery, canned foods, and preserves as an acidity regulator and flavouring. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered very safe, being a normal part of human metabolism.

name: Sodium Citrate
description: The sodium salt of citric acid, used as a flavouring and preservative, considered safe.
detailed_description: Sodium citrate is the sodium salt of citric acid, used as an acidity regulator, emulsifier, and flavouring agent. It is commonly found in gelatin desserts, processed cheese, carbonated beverages, and pharmaceutical preparations. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Potassium Citrates
description: Potassium salts of citric acid used as acidity regulators and stabilisers, considered safe.
detailed_description: Potassium citrates are the potassium salts of citric acid, used as acidity regulators, stabilisers, and buffering agents in food products. They are commonly found in jams, jellies, confectionery, and evaporated milk. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe for general consumption.

name: Phosphoric Acid
description: Used in cola drinks, excessive intake may affect calcium absorption and bone health.
detailed_description: Phosphoric acid is a mineral acid used as an acidity regulator and flavouring agent, most notably providing the sharp tangy taste in cola-type beverages. It is also found in processed cheese and some jams. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some research suggests that excessive phosphoric acid intake from soft drinks may be associated with reduced calcium absorption and potential effects on bone health.
potential_risks: Some epidemiological studies associate high cola consumption with reduced bone mineral density
Excessive phosphate intake may interfere with calcium absorption and phosphorus-calcium balance

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of phosphoric acid and phosphates (E 338-341, E 343, E 450-452)

name: Sodium Phosphates
description: Emulsifiers and stabilisers, excessive phosphate intake may affect kidney function.
detailed_description: Sodium phosphates are a group of sodium salts of phosphoric acid used as emulsifiers, stabilisers, and acidity regulators. They are commonly found in processed cheese, processed meats, and baking powders. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though EFSA has noted that combined phosphate intake from all food sources may exceed the acceptable daily intake in some population groups, which may be a concern for individuals with impaired kidney function.
potential_risks: Excessive phosphate intake may be a concern for individuals with kidney disease
EFSA noted that combined dietary phosphate intake may exceed the ADI in some population groups

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of phosphoric acid and phosphates (E 338-341, E 343, E 450-452)

name: Potassium Phosphates
description: Used as acidity regulators and emulsifiers, considered safe in normal food amounts.
detailed_description: Potassium phosphates are a group of potassium salts of phosphoric acid used as acidity regulators, emulsifiers, and stabilisers. They are found in coffee creamers, processed cheese, and powdered foods. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe in normal food amounts, though general guidance on phosphate intake applies.

name: Sodium Alginate
description: A natural thickener and gelling agent derived from brown seaweed, considered safe.
detailed_description: Sodium alginate is a natural polysaccharide extracted from brown seaweed (kelp), used as a thickener, gelling agent, and stabiliser. It is commonly found in ice cream, desserts, salad dressings, and as a coating on frozen fish. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Carrageenan
description: A thickener from seaweed, some studies suggest it may cause gut inflammation.
regulation: UK FSA / Assimilated Regulation (EC) No. 1333/2008 — under review
detailed_description: Carrageenan is a natural polysaccharide extracted from red seaweed, widely used as a thickener, gelling agent, and stabiliser in dairy products, plant-based milks, deli meats, and desserts. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits. Some in vitro and animal studies have suggested it may promote gut inflammation, though regulatory bodies including EFSA have concluded that food-grade carrageenan is safe at current usage levels, and the topic remains under ongoing scientific review.
potential_risks: Some animal and in vitro studies suggest degraded carrageenan may promote gut inflammation
A few researchers have raised concerns about potential effects on the gut microbiome
Under periodic review by EFSA due to ongoing scientific debate

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of carrageenan (E 407) and processed Eucheuma seaweed (E 407a)
Review of harmful gastrointestinal effects of carrageenan in animal experiments

name: Locust Bean Gum
description: A natural thickener from carob seeds, commonly used in ice cream and dairy, considered safe.
detailed_description: Locust bean gum (carob gum) is a natural galactomannan polysaccharide extracted from the seeds of the carob tree (Ceratonia siliqua). It is commonly used as a thickener and stabiliser in ice cream, cream cheese, and bakery fillings. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Guar Gum
description: A natural thickener from guar beans, considered safe in food amounts.
detailed_description: Guar gum is a natural polysaccharide extracted from the seeds of the guar plant (Cyamopsis tetragonoloba), primarily grown in India. It is widely used as a thickener and stabiliser in ice cream, sauces, soups, and gluten-free baked goods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe in normal food amounts.

name: Acacia Gum
description: A natural gum from acacia trees used as an emulsifier and stabiliser, considered safe.
detailed_description: Acacia gum (gum arabic) is a natural gum harvested from the sap of Acacia senegal and Acacia seyal trees, primarily from the Sahel region of Africa. It is used as an emulsifier, stabiliser, and thickener in confectionery, soft drinks, and wine. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, being one of the oldest and most widely used natural food additives.

name: Xanthan Gum
description: A common thickener produced by fermentation, considered safe.
detailed_description: Xanthan gum is a polysaccharide produced by bacterial fermentation of glucose or sucrose by the bacterium Xanthomonas campestris. It is one of the most common thickeners in the food industry, found in salad dressings, sauces, gluten-free products, and ice cream. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Sorbitol
description: A sugar alcohol found naturally in fruits, safe but may cause digestive discomfort in large amounts.
detailed_description: Sorbitol is a sugar alcohol (polyol) found naturally in apples, pears, and stone fruits, and is produced commercially from glucose. It is commonly used as a sweetener in sugar-free confectionery, chewing gum, and diabetic products. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, though products containing more than 10% added polyols must carry a warning that excessive consumption may produce laxative effects.

name: Mannitol
description: A sugar alcohol used as a sweetener, safe but may cause digestive issues in excess.
detailed_description: Mannitol is a sugar alcohol found naturally in seaweed, mushrooms, and tree bark, and is produced commercially by hydrogenation of fructose. It is used as a sweetener and anti-caking agent in sugar-free confectionery, chewing gum, and pharmaceutical tablets. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, though excessive consumption may cause gastrointestinal discomfort.

name: Glycerol
description: A naturally occurring sugar alcohol used as a sweetener and humectant, considered safe.
detailed_description: Glycerol (glycerine) is a naturally occurring sugar alcohol that is a component of all animal and vegetable fats and oils. It is widely used as a humectant, sweetener, and solvent in confectionery, cake icings, and soft drinks. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, being a normal metabolic intermediate in the human body.

name: Pectin
description: A natural gelling agent found in fruit, completely safe and used in jams.
detailed_description: Pectin is a naturally occurring polysaccharide found in the cell walls of fruits, particularly apples and citrus peel. It is used as a gelling agent, thickener, and stabiliser in jams, jellies, marmalades, and fruit-based desserts. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered completely safe, also being valued as a source of soluble dietary fibre.

name: Diphosphates
description: Raising agents used in baking, excessive phosphate intake may affect mineral absorption.
detailed_description: Diphosphates (pyrophosphates) are a group of phosphate salts used as raising agents in baking powders, self-raising flour, and cake mixes, and as emulsifiers in processed cheese and meat products. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though EFSA has noted that total phosphate intake from all food additive sources may exceed the acceptable daily intake in some population groups.
potential_risks: EFSA noted that combined dietary phosphate intake may exceed the group ADI in some populations
Excessive phosphate intake may interfere with calcium absorption and affect bone health

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of phosphoric acid and phosphates (E 338-341, E 343, E 450-452)

name: Triphosphates
description: Used in processed meats, excessive phosphate intake is linked to cardiovascular concerns.
detailed_description: Triphosphates are phosphate salts used as water-retention agents and emulsifiers in processed meats, seafood, and cheese products. They help improve texture and moisture in cooked ham, sausages, and surimi. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some research has associated excessive dietary phosphate intake with cardiovascular concerns, particularly in individuals with kidney disease.
potential_risks: Excessive phosphate intake has been associated with cardiovascular concerns in some epidemiological studies
EFSA noted that total phosphate additive intake may exceed the ADI in some population groups
May be a particular concern for individuals with impaired kidney function

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of phosphoric acid and phosphates (E 338-341, E 343, E 450-452)

name: Cellulose
description: Plant fibre used as a bulking agent, considered safe.
detailed_description: Cellulose is a natural polysaccharide that forms the structural component of plant cell walls, making it the most abundant organic compound on Earth. It is used as a bulking agent, anti-caking agent, and thickener in grated cheese, bread, and low-calorie foods. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, being an insoluble dietary fibre.

name: Mono- and Diglycerides
description: Common emulsifiers made from fats, considered safe in normal food amounts.
detailed_description: Mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids are among the most widely used emulsifiers in the food industry, produced from glycerol and natural fatty acids. They are found in bread, margarine, ice cream, cakes, and many other processed foods. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe, as they are digested and metabolised in the same way as natural fats.

name: DATEM
description: An emulsifier used in bread to improve texture, considered safe.
detailed_description: DATEM (diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides) is an emulsifier widely used in bread and bakery products to improve dough strength, texture, and volume. It is also found in some confectionery and coffee whiteners. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: PGPR
description: An emulsifier made from castor oil, used in chocolate — safe but highly processed.
detailed_description: PGPR (polyglycerol polyricinoleate) is an emulsifier derived from castor oil and glycerol, primarily used in chocolate and chocolate-flavoured coatings to reduce viscosity and improve flow properties. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, with EFSA setting an ADI of 25 mg/kg body weight per day. While it is considered safe within these limits, it is a highly processed additive that some consumers prefer to avoid.
potential_risks: A highly processed additive derived from castor oil, which some consumers prefer to avoid
EFSA has set a specific ADI, indicating that intake should be monitored within limits

name: Sodium Carbonates
description: Baking soda — a natural raising agent, completely safe.
detailed_description: Sodium carbonates include sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate (baking soda), and sodium sesquicarbonate, used as raising agents, acidity regulators, and anti-caking agents. They are found in baked goods, confectionery, and effervescent drinks. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered completely safe at normal usage levels.

name: Ammonium Carbonates
description: A raising agent used in baking, breaks down during cooking and is considered safe.
detailed_description: Ammonium carbonates are raising agents that decompose during baking to release carbon dioxide, ammonia, and water, leaving no residue in the finished product. They are traditionally used in flat baked goods such as gingerbread, crackers, and biscuits. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe, as they fully decompose during the cooking process.

name: Calcium Sulphate
description: A natural mineral used as a firming agent and flour treatment agent, considered safe.
detailed_description: Calcium sulphate is a naturally occurring mineral (gypsum) used as a firming agent, flour treatment agent, and coagulant in tofu production. It is commonly found in bread, canned vegetables, and tofu. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Silicon Dioxide
description: A natural anti-caking agent found in sand and quartz, considered safe in food amounts.
detailed_description: Silicon dioxide (silica) is a naturally occurring mineral found abundantly in sand, quartz, and many plants. It is used as an anti-caking agent in powdered foods such as spices, salt, coffee whiteners, and powdered sugar. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe in food amounts, as it passes through the body without being absorbed.

name: Talc
description: A mineral anti-caking agent, food-grade talc is generally considered safe but some concerns exist about contamination.
detailed_description: Talc is a naturally occurring mineral (hydrated magnesium silicate) used as an anti-caking agent and surface coating in confectionery, chewing gum, and rice. Food-grade talc is purified to strict standards. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, though some concerns have been raised about potential asbestos contamination in talc from certain geological sources, which is why food-grade specifications require rigorous purity testing.
potential_risks: Some natural talc deposits may contain traces of asbestos, though food-grade talc must meet strict purity requirements
Ongoing scientific discussion about long-term safety, primarily related to non-food-grade talc exposure

sources.title: EFSA re-evaluation of talc (E 553b) as a food additive

name: Fatty Acids
description: Naturally occurring fats used as emulsifiers and anti-caking agents, considered safe.
detailed_description: Fatty acids (stearic acid, palmitic acid, and others) are naturally occurring components of animal and vegetable fats. They are used as anti-caking agents, emulsifiers, and glazing agents in confectionery, baked goods, and dietary supplements. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe, as they are identical to the fatty acids found naturally in food.

name: Glutamic Acid
description: A naturally occurring amino acid and flavour enhancer found in many foods, considered safe.
detailed_description: Glutamic acid is a naturally occurring amino acid that is one of the most abundant in nature, found in high concentrations in tomatoes, Parmesan cheese, mushrooms, and soy sauce. It provides the characteristic 'umami' (savoury) taste. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, being a natural component of proteins in the human body.

name: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
description: A flavour enhancer found naturally in tomatoes and cheese, safe for most people.
detailed_description: Monosodium glutamate (MSG) is the sodium salt of glutamic acid, used as a flavour enhancer to provide umami (savoury) taste. It is commonly found in crisps, instant noodles, soups, sauces, and Chinese cuisine. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits, and extensive scientific reviews by EFSA, the FDA, and other bodies have concluded it is safe for the general population at normal dietary levels, though a small number of individuals report sensitivity.

name: Disodium Guanylate
description: A flavour enhancer often used alongside MSG, considered safe.
detailed_description: Disodium guanylate is a flavour enhancer that works synergistically with MSG to intensify umami (savoury) taste. It is commonly found in crisps, instant noodles, snack seasonings, and stock cubes. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Disodium Inosinate
description: A flavour enhancer often used alongside MSG, derived from meat or fish, considered safe.
detailed_description: Disodium inosinate is a flavour enhancer that works synergistically with MSG and disodium guanylate to intensify savoury umami flavour. It is commonly found in crisps, instant noodles, stock cubes, and savoury snacks. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Dimethyl Polysiloxane
description: An anti-foaming agent used in cooking oils and frying, considered safe in food amounts.
detailed_description: Dimethyl polysiloxane (polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS) is a silicone-based anti-foaming agent used in cooking oils, frying fats, and some beverages to prevent excessive foaming. It is commonly added to deep-frying oil in fast food outlets and found in some soft drinks and juices. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe in food amounts, as it is inert and passes through the body without being absorbed.

name: Beeswax
description: A natural wax used as a glazing agent on confectionery and fruit, considered safe.
detailed_description: Beeswax is a natural wax produced by honey bees, used as a glazing agent and coating on confectionery, fresh fruit, nuts, and coffee beans to provide a shiny appearance and protective barrier. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption, having been used in food for centuries.

name: Carnauba Wax
description: A natural plant wax used as a glazing agent, considered safe.
detailed_description: Carnauba wax is a natural wax obtained from the leaves of the Brazilian palm tree Copernicia prunifera. It is used as a glazing agent on confectionery, chewing gum, fresh fruit, and pharmaceutical tablets to provide a glossy coating. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Shellac
description: A natural resin secreted by lac insects, used as a glazing agent on confectionery and pills, considered safe.
detailed_description: Shellac is a natural resin secreted by the female lac insect (Kerria lacca), primarily harvested in India and Thailand. It is used as a glazing agent on confectionery, chocolate, citrus fruits, and pharmaceutical tablets to provide a shiny, protective coating. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Aspartame
description: An artificial sweetener that is safe at approved levels but remains controversial.
regulation: UK FSA / Assimilated Regulation (EC) No. 1333/2008 — EFSA ADI: 40mg/kg/day
detailed_description: Aspartame is a low-calorie artificial sweetener approximately 200 times sweeter than sugar, used in diet soft drinks, sugar-free confectionery, tabletop sweeteners, and many reduced-calorie products. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits with an ADI of 40 mg/kg body weight per day. EFSA re-evaluated aspartame in 2013 and concluded it is safe at current exposure levels, though IARC classified it as 'possibly carcinogenic to humans' (Group 2B) in 2023, noting limited evidence; the topic remains an area of active scientific discussion.
potential_risks: IARC classified aspartame as 'possibly carcinogenic to humans' (Group 2B) in 2023, though noting limited evidence
Individuals with the rare genetic condition phenylketonuria (PKU) must avoid aspartame as it contains phenylalanine
Some observational studies have reported associations with headaches and other symptoms, though controlled trials have not consistently confirmed these

sources.title: EFSA scientific opinion on the re-evaluation of aspartame (E 951)

name: Sucralose
description: An artificial sweetener, generally considered safe but some studies question gut health effects.
detailed_description: Sucralose is a zero-calorie artificial sweetener approximately 600 times sweeter than sugar, made by chlorinating sucrose. It is commonly found in diet drinks, sugar-free confectionery, baked goods, and tabletop sweeteners. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits with an ADI of 15 mg/kg body weight per day, though some recent studies have raised questions about potential effects on gut bacteria and glucose metabolism.
potential_risks: Some studies suggest sucralose may alter the composition of gut microbiota
A few studies have reported potential effects on glucose and insulin responses, though findings are inconsistent

name: Steviol Glycosides
description: A natural sweetener from stevia leaves, considered a safer alternative to artificial sweeteners.
detailed_description: Steviol glycosides are natural high-intensity sweeteners extracted from the leaves of the Stevia rebaudiana plant, native to South America. They are approximately 200-300 times sweeter than sugar and are used in beverages, confectionery, and tabletop sweeteners. They are permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and are considered safe, offering a plant-derived alternative to synthetic sweeteners.

name: Maltitol
description: A sugar alcohol used in sugar-free products, safe but may cause digestive discomfort.
detailed_description: Maltitol is a sugar alcohol (polyol) produced by hydrogenation of maltose, with about 75-90% of the sweetness of sugar but significantly fewer calories. It is commonly found in sugar-free chocolate, confectionery, chewing gum, and ice cream. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe, though products containing more than 10% added polyols must carry a warning about potential laxative effects from excessive consumption.

name: Oxidised Starch
description: A modified starch used as a thickener and emulsifier, considered safe.
detailed_description: Oxidised starch is a chemically modified starch produced by treating native starch with an oxidising agent. It is used as a thickener, emulsifier, and binder in sauces, confectionery coatings, and bakery products. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Acetylated Starch
description: A modified starch used as a thickener and stabiliser, considered safe.
detailed_description: Acetylated starch is a modified starch produced by treating native starch with acetic anhydride or vinyl acetate. It is used as a thickener, stabiliser, and texturiser in canned foods, frozen foods, and sauces. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Acetylated Distarch Adipate
description: A modified starch used as a thickener and stabiliser, commonly found in sauces, considered safe.
detailed_description: Acetylated distarch adipate is a modified starch produced by cross-linking starch with adipic anhydride and then acetylating it. It is one of the most commonly used modified starches, found in sauces, gravies, soups, yoghurts, and ready meals. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Hydroxy Propyl Distarch Phosphate
description: A modified starch used as a thickener in many processed foods, considered safe.
detailed_description: Hydroxy propyl distarch phosphate is a modified starch that has been cross-linked and hydroxypropylated to improve its stability under heat, acid, and freeze-thaw conditions. It is widely used in ready meals, sauces, soups, and dairy desserts. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.

name: Starch Sodium Octenyl Succinate
description: A modified starch used as an emulsifier and stabiliser, considered safe.
detailed_description: Starch sodium octenyl succinate (OSA starch) is a modified starch with emulsifying properties, produced by treating starch with octenyl succinic anhydride. It is commonly used in beverage emulsions, encapsulated flavourings, and salad dressings. It is permitted under EU/UK regulation at established limits and is considered safe for general consumption.
